coredata.dc:title: Surface plasmon resonance measurement of arsenic in low concentration using polypyrrole-graphene quantum dots layer
coredata.dc:creator: Sadrolhosseini, Amir Reza
Shafie, Suhaidi
Rashid, Suraya Abdul
Mahdi, Mohd Adzir
coredata.dc:description: A surface plasmon resonance method based on a prismatic configuration can be considered as a versatile accurate technique, which can be utilized in detecting chemical toxins. In this study, layers of polypyrrole-graphene dots nanocomposite were produced at different deposition times using the electrodeposition method. Characterization methods such as the field emission-scanning microscopy, X-ray diffraction spectroscopy, and the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy were employed to obtain and verify the morphology and chemical elements present in the composite layer. The thickness and refractive index of polypyrrole-graphene quantum dots layer ranged between 3.2 nm and 65.2 nm and 1.6895+i 0.112 to 1.6076+i 0.163, respectively. The layer was utilized to measure the low concentration of arsenic (As+3) ions in the range of 0.005–10 ppm and the results have been compared with the response of sensor in the presence of mercury (Hg+2) and lead (Pb+2) ions. However, it must be noted that the sensor’s limit was 0.005 ppm, and the sensitivity of arsenic detection was larger than that of mercury and lead ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Arsenic ion
Mercury ion
Lead ion
Surface plasmon resonance
Polypyrrole-graphene quantum dots

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) The SPR setup contains a He-Ne laser, a high index prism, a polarizer, a pinhole, a chopper, a precision rotation stage, a photodetector, a lock in amplifier. b) The ray tracing in the prism.

a) The XRD spectrum of GQD and PPy-GQD; the FT-IR spectrum of b) GQD and c) PPy-GQD; d) the FE-SEM image of PPy-GQD nanocomposite layer and e) the FE-SEM image of GQD which distributed on the surface of PPy; f) TEM image for demonstration of GQD morphology and g) analysis of image (f).

a) The SPR signal related to different thickness of PPy-GQD nanocomposite layer in the range of 3.2 nm to 65.2 nm; b) the SPR signal related to PPy-GQD sensing layer with the 12.3 nm thickness for baseline at 55.580°.
a) Variation of thickness with time; variation of (b) real and (c) imaginary parts of refractive index of PPy-GQD nanocomposite layer with thickness.
The sensogram for detecting of a) arsenic, b) mercury, c) lead ions and d) The variation of the resonance angle shift with different concentrations of arsenic, mercury, and lead ions.
Variation of resonance angle shift with concentration of As+3 and As+5.
a) The sensogram related to mixture of arsenic, mercury, and lead in 0.5 ppm and b) variation of resonance angle shift with concentration of As(III).

a) Sensogram for detection of the arsenic ion using polypyrrole layer, b) variation of the resonance angle shift for the detection of arsenic, mercury and lead with the different concentration of ions.
The molecular structure of PPy-GQD and it interacted with As ions.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: n and k are the real and imaginary parts of refractive index of PPy-GQD.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Pertinent parameters for binding of arsenic, mercury and lead ions to PPy-GQD layer.
The concentrations of ions before and after the experiments.

head.ce:title: Surface plasmon resonance measurement of arsenic in low concentration using polypyrrole-graphene quantum dots layer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Functional Device Laboratory (FDL), Institute of Advanced Technology (ITMA), Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400UPM Serdang, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Functional Device Laboratory (FDL), Institute of Advanced Technology (ITMA), Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400UPM Serdang, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Material Processing and Technology Laboratory (MPTL), Institute of Advanced Technology (ITMA), Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400UPM Serdang, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Material Processing and Technology Laboratory (MPTL), Institute of Advanced Technology (ITMA), Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400UPM Serdang, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Centre of Excellence for Wireless and Photonics Network (WIPNET), Faculty of Engineering, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400UPM Selangor, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Centre of Excellence for Wireless and Photonics Network (WIPNET)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Centre of Excellence for Wireless and Photonics Network (WIPNET), Faculty of Engineering, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400UPM Selangor, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A surface plasmon resonance method based on a prismatic configuration can be considered as a versatile accurate technique, which can be utilized in detecting chemical toxins. In this study, layers of polypyrrole-graphene dots nanocomposite were produced at different deposition times using the electrodeposition method. Characterization methods such as the field emission-scanning microscopy, X-ray diffraction spectroscopy, and the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy were employed to obtain and verify the morphology and chemical elements present in the composite layer. The thickness and refractive index of polypyrrole-graphene quantum dots layer ranged between 3.2 nm and 65.2 nm and 1.6895+i 0.112 to 1.6076+i 0.163, respectively. The layer was utilized to measure the low concentration of arsenic (As+3) ions in the range of 0.005–10 ppm and the results have been compared with the response of sensor in the presence of mercury (Hg+2) and lead (Pb+2) ions. However, it must be noted that the sensor’s limit was 0.005 ppm, and the sensitivity of arsenic detection was larger than that of mercury and lead ions.

ce:section.ce:para: The elements such as arsenic (As), mercury (Hg) and lead (Pb) possess toxic properties. The atomic numbers of arsenic, mercury, and lead are 33, 80, and 82, respectively. As can be seen from the periodic table, arsenic is in group 5 of the periodic table, and mercury and lead are heavy metals from the transition-metals group. Toxic elements contamination of natural products, beverages, and drinking water can be regularly found [1]. Arsenic components and arsenic metal can be detected in their organic and inorganic forms and can be dissolved in water and aqueous solutions. The toxicity of arsenic and arsenic-components are attributed to their valence states and their solubility. Several oxidation states of arsenic can be found in the environment including +5, −3, 0, and +3 [2,3]. An arsenate, arsenic sulfide, metal arsenates and arsenides are the common oxidation of arsenic samples. Trivalent arsenic (As+3) and pentavalent arsenic (As+5) are present in drinking water and natural water as inorganic oxyanions. Moreover, it must be noted that organic arsenic such as dimethylarsinic acid (DMA), and trimethylarsine oxide (TMAO) can be generated by biological-activities of organisms in the surrounding water [2]. Liver, lung, prostate, and kidney cancer alongside hyperpigmentation of the skin, or keratosis of the feet or hands, which is a chronic health problem, are some of the side-effects of exposure and ingestion of arsenic [4]. Numerous techniques and instruments are employed in order to detect the presence of arsenic and measure its concentration in water and other liquid solutions. The approved and most prominent techniques include; atomic absorption spectroscopy [3], atomic emission spectroscopy (AES) [3], atomic fluorescence spectrometry [5], coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [2], ICP-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [2], anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV), high-performance liquid chromatography [6], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [7], and surface plasmon resonance [1]. The most recent utilized methods for arsenic removal include ion exchange [8], coagulation [9], reverse osmosis [10], adsorption [11], and electrocoagulation [12]. In order to detect and recognize arsenic ions by nanoparticles such as silver nanoparticles, the colourimetric detection method could be utilized [3].

The modify gold nanoparticle with n-alkanethiols in different chain lengths as a different immobilization strategy was used to improve the gold surface for detection of arsenic and the sensor limit was reported about 5 ppm [13].

Forzani el at. used the thiol-containing organic compounds as sensor probes for detection of arsenic in the groundwater and the sensor limit was reported 10 ppb [14]. Other method such as cyclic voltammetry was used to measure and detect the arsenic based on 3-nitro-L-tyrosine and the sensor can detect the concentration in the range of 0.1 µM to 100 µM [15].

Mercury and lead toxic heavy-metals are not only harmful to the environment but can be found in water and food chains, serving as lethal toxins to organisms [16]. Doses of mercury or lead can cause severe damage to the kidneys, bones, brain, and liver [17]. It is known that atomic- absorption spectroscopy, coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), fluorescence spectrometry, cold vapour atomic absorption spectroscopy (CVAAS), X-ray fluorescence (XRF), and the surface plasmon resonance technique [18] are the main analytical methods which could be employed in order to measure low concentrations of mercury and lead ions.

The SPR sensor is an accurate and versatile technique to evaluate the chemical and biochemical molecules. The SPR phenomena is due to optical properties of dielectric and metal layers [19] and it depends on charge density oscillation at the interface of metal and dielectric layer. Hence, the SPR signal is sensitive to variation of optical properties of chemical and biochemical materials such as heavy metal, DNA, and lipid [19]. Therefore, the surface plasmon sensor have a high potential to recognize the toxic chemical such as arsenic. The SPR signals can analyse based on Fresnel’s theory [19] and many methods were presented for analysis the SPR signals such as matrix method [20] and effective-medium theory (EMT) for 2D structure [21]. Gold layer is the common metal layer that has been used to design the SPR sensor and to enhance the sensitivity and selectivity of the SPR sensor, the sensing layer such as nanolayer, polymer and polymer composite have been coated on the surface of gold layer. Recently, the carbon-based nanomaterials including graphene oxide, graphene, graphene quantum dots use to improve the gold layer.

Graphene quantum dots (GQD) is a zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterial. GQD have superior physical- and chemical- properties in comparison to other quantum dots, such as high photostability, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and high confinement and edge effects [22]. Furthermore, due to a more abundant atom quantity at the edges of GQDs in comparison to graphene and other nanomaterials, GQD has a higher electron-mobility and a quicker reaction rate with other molecules. However, it must be noted that this may be due to the fact that the surface to volume ratio in GQD is larger than other nanoparticles [23]. GQD possess variation in band gaps, which attributes to alteration in its electron-transport ability [24] and photoluminescence properties with variation in the size of the GQDs. GQD’s ability in molecular absorption and variation in optical and electrical properties are the most critical reasons for their utilization in optical and electrical sensors [25]. The GQD have particle size less than 10 nm and fast electron transfer kinetics [24], which enhances adsorption of molecules. GQD have unique plasmonic structure [26] and tuneable surface chemistry [24], and high potential for sensor application because, GQD have a unique property such as low intrinsic toxicity, high stability, excellent solubility and easier binding of their surface with receptors [27].

Over the last decade, Polypyrrole, polythiophene, and polyaniline were the foremost used conductive polymers for electronic devices, optical and electrical sensors, as well as biosensors and supercapacitors. Due to its double-bond system, conjugated electron, and the electron alternating between single bonds in the aromatic ring [28], polypyrrole (PPy) is a potent electrical conductor. It also possesses high thermal stability, high chemical resistivity, and environmental stability [29]. In addition, PPy is utilized for micro- and nano-electronic devices, biosensors, batteries, and biomedical equipment [30]. It has been reported that PPy may also be used to detect toxic heavy- metals [31], glucose [32], and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [33]. In accordance with the literature the PPy-GQD was used in the different application such as supercapacitor [34], dopamin detection [35], glucose detection [32], and bisphenol A in water [36]. Therefore, the polypyrrole-graphene quantum dots have high potential to improve the gold layer for enhancing the selectivity and sensitivity of the SPR sensor to detect the chemical and biochemical elements.

The sensitive, accurate, and fast method are the interest subject to measure the low concentration of toxic chemical. Hence, in this research, the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensor was applied to measure and detect the low concentration of arsenic, mercury, and lead ions using polypyrrole graphene quantum dots. The main idea is based on improvement the surface of gold layer for application of the SPR sensor using polypyrrole-graphene quantum dots to enhance the sensitivity and response time. Therefore, in this study, the polypyrrole-graphene quantum dots nanocomposite layer was prepared on the surface of gold-coated glass slide. The structure, functional group and morphology of the prepared layer was analysed using X-ray spectroscopy, Fourier transform- infrared spectroscopy, and Field Emission Scanning Microscopy. The PPy-GQD nanocomposite layer (sensing layer) was utilized to detect the presence and measure the low concentrations of arsenic, mercury, and lead ions in water by using the SPR technique to evaluate the potential of PPy-GQD for abortion of toxic elements.

Pure Sodium dodecylbenzensulfonate (SDBS), graphene quantum dots, HgSO4 (ACS reagent, >99%), PbSO4 (trace metal basis, 99.995%), and pyrrole (FCC, FG, >98%) in their commercial form, alongside a standard arsenic solution with a concentration of 1000 mg/L were purchased from the Sigma-Aldrich company. High pure As2O3, and As2O5 were purchased from PubChem company. The graphene quantum dots in the 1000 ppm concentration with 8 nm particle size was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich company.

A liquid index-match, high refractive-index prism (ZF 52, n = 1.83956, A = 60°) Foctek), and a reference electrode (saturated calomel electros (SCE)) were purchased from the NewPort optical company, Focteck, and BASi Company, respectively.

To prepare the As+3 aqueous solutions, 1 g of As2O3 was dissolved in 1 L of deionized distilled water at temperature of 30°. The solution was sonicated for 1 h to obtain the homogeneous solution. The prepared solution contained 1000 ppm of As+3 ions. Subsequently, the low concentrations of ions solution, being 0.005, 0.05, 0.5, 5, and 10 ppm were separately prepared by the systematic dilution of 1000 ppm of As2O3 in deionized distilled water [20].

To obtain the As+5 solution, 1 g of As2O5 was dissolved in 1 L of deionized distilled water at temperature of 30°. The solution of 1000 ppm of arsenic (As+5) was systematically diluted in deionized distilled water for the preparation of low concentrations (0.005, 0.05, 0.5, 5, and 10 ppm) of the arsenic ion (As+5).

To prepare the Hg ion solution, 1 g of HgSO4 was dissolved in 1 L of deionized distilled water at temperature of 30° to prepare the Hg+2 solution. To obtain the homogeneous solution, the solution was stirred for 1 h at temperature of 30°. The prepared solution contained 1000 ppm of Hg ions (Hg+2). Subsequently, the low concentration of Hg+2 (0.005, 0.05, 0.5, 5, and 10 ppm) solutions were obtained by the systematic dilution of 1000 ppm of HgSO4 in deionized distilled water [17].

1 g of PbSO4 was dissolved in 1 L of deionized distilled water at temperature of 25° to prepare the Pb+2 solution. To obtain the homogeneous solution, the solution was stirred for 1 h at temperature of 25°. The prepared solution contained 1000 ppm of Pb ions (Pb+2). Subsequently, the low concentration of Pb+2 solution, being 0.005, 0.05, 0.5,5, and 10 ppm were separately prepared by the systematic dilution of 1000 ppm of PbSO4 in deionized distilled water [17].

The electrodeposition of PPy-GQD composite layer was carried out on the surface of a gold-coated glass slide. The gold layer was coated on the surface of the thin glass slide using the sputtering coating technique. The final thickness of the gold layer was 46.6 nm.

The PPy-GQD nanocomposite layer was fabricated via electrochemical polymerization of pure and distilled pyrrole in the presence of graphene quantum dots. The mixture of graphene quantum dots and the sodium dodecylbenzensulfonate (SDBS) was prepared with a 1:3 ratio and was sonicated for 3 h. The monomer solution included 0.1 M pyrrole, 100 ppm graphene quantum dots, and SDBS. The solution was stirred before the beginning of the experiment. The polymerization of pyrrole was carried out at the constant potential of 0.83 V. The reference electrode, working electrode, and counter electrode were saturated calomel electrodes (SCE), graphite electrodes, and gold-coated glass slide, respectively. The electrodeposition of PPy-GQD was performed at different times including 5, 7, 10, 13, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, and 40 s. The prepared layers were analysed by X-ray diffraction spectroscopy (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and field emission scaning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). The thickness of the formed layers was measured using a profilemeter.

The PPy sensing layer was fabricated using electrochemical deposition technique on a gold-coated glass slide as reported in [17]. Briefly, the electrochemical deposition of PPy was carried out using a potentiostat (Autolab PGSTAT 101). The polymers were potentiostatically synthesized in a solution containing 0.3 M Pyrrole (pre-distilled), 0.1 M p-toluene sulfonate (P-TS) dopant at room temperature.

Fig. 1 illustrates the SPR homemade setup. Through the utilization of a liquid-index match, the PPy-GQD was coated on the surface of a gold-coated thin glass slide (SF52, FocTek, nslide , =1.83956, thickness is 0.1 mm) which was attached to a prism with a refractive index of 1.83956 (SF52, FocTeck) on its uncoated side. The p-polarized He-Ne laser beam (λ = 633 nm) enabled the utilization of a polarizer, which in turn allowed the laser to be focused onto the prism. The prism was rotated up to 30° with the step about 0.003°. Once the prism stopped, the laser intensity was momentarily registered using the photodetector and the SPR signal was achieved. The SPR signals were analysed using Fresnel’s theory in order to obtain the resonance angles and the resonance angles’ shift [1,18].

Fig. 1(b) displays the ray-tracing of the laser beam within the prism, while A represents the prism apex angle, and θ1 is the incident angle of the He-Ne laser beam with respect to the prism. The angle of reflection of the laser beam from the gold layer is θ2 and could be calculated as follows [20]:
(1)
θ 2
= A - arcsin
n 0
n p
sin
θ 1
Where
n 0
and
n p
are the refractive indices of air and prism, respectively.

Surface plasmon resonance is an optical effect, which corresponds to the charge density oscillation at the interface of the conductive layers and the dielectric medium. The SPR phenomena can be described via Feresnel’s theory. In this study, the gold-layer was sandwiched between the glass and the PPy-GQD, which acted as a sensing layer. According to the electromagnetism theory, the reflection coefficient (
r p
) of the laser beam can be obtained by Fresnel’ theory as follows:
(2)
r p
=
E i
E r
Where
E i
and
E r
are the amplitude of the incident and reflected electrical fields of the laser beam, respectively. The reflection coefficient of the laser beam from the N layers system considered can be obtained, if in accordance with the boundary condition for the magnetic, and electrical fields of the laser beam at the interface between the layers, as follows [37]:
(3)
r =
m 21
+
m 22
τ 2
-
m 11
τ 0
-
m 12
τ 2
τ 0
m 21
+
m 22
τ 2
+
m 11
τ 0
+
m 12
τ 2
τ 0
,
where mij is matrix layer elements and the
τ N
is defined as follows:
(4)
τ N
=
n N
cos
θ
t N
ε 0
μ 0

In this equation,
ε 0
and
μ 0
are the susceptibility and permittivity of the medium used, respectively. The matrix of each layer is
(5)
M =
cos δ
- i
sin δ
τ N
- i
τ N
sin δ
cos δ
,
δ =
2 π
λ
d
n N
cos
θ
t N
Where
n N
,
δ
,
d
,
θ
t N
and
λ
are the refractive index of Nth layer, the phase shift, thickness of the layer, the refractive angle of Nth layer and wavelength, respectively. If the matrix of each layer is multiplied together, the matrix of the N layer system will be derived as follows [20,37]:
(6)
M =
M 1
M 2
M 3
. . . . . . . . . . . . .
M N
,
M =
m 11
m 12
m 21
m 22
,
The reflectance is R = r × r.

The reflectance is a function of the wavelengths, refractive indices of the gold, sensing layer, and analyte alongside the thicknesses of the gold and sensing layers. The SPR signal is registered in the SPR sensor, and as the thickness, refractive indices of the gold layer, and the prism are known, an accurate value for the resonance angle and the optical parameter of the sensing layer can be obtained by minimizing the summation of the theoretical (RTh ) and the experimental (REx ) values of the reflectance. The equation is then as follows:
(7)
Ω =
∑ θ
[
R
Th
( θ
, n
) -
R
Ex
( θ , n )
]
.

The structure of the PPy-GQD nanocomposite layer was assessed via an X-ray diffraction (XRD) device with a Shimadzu diffractometer (model XRD 6000) and Cu, Ka (0.154 nm) radiation in order to generate the XRD spectrum.

The concentrations of arsenic, mercury, and lead ions were measured with the use of inductively-coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS; Optima 2000 DV; Perkin Elmer). The concentrations of the samples were measured prior to the experiment, with an accuracy of about 0.001 mg/L.

The PPy-GQD nanocomposite layer was synthesized using the electrodeposition technique. Fig. 2 illustrates the XRD spectrum, FT-IR spectra, and the FE-SEM images of the prepared sample. Fig. 2(a) displays the XRD pattern for pure GQD, pure polypyrrole layer and PPy-GQD layer sensor. The XRD spectrum for pure PPy and pure GQD show the broad peak at 21.3° [38] and 25.1° [39], respectively. The peak at 25.1° corresponded to (002) crystalline plane of graphene [39] in GQD and the broad peak at 21.3° related to amorphous polypyrrole and its related to scattering the polypyrrole chains [38].The XRD spectrum for PPy-GQD sensing layer depicts the broad peak, which experienced an overlap, appeared at 25° and 21.3° to the centre. The broad peak confirmed the polypyrrole form [38] in the composite and the peak at 25° authenticates GQD formed in the PPy-GQD nanocomposite layer [40]. Furthermore, the peak at 21.3° was related to the presence of polypyrrole in the sample [39].

Fig. 2(b) and 2(c) display the FT-IR spectrum of pure GQD and the combined PPy-GQD, respectively. The main peaks occurred at 3320 cm−1 and 1641.3 cm−1, which were attributed to the stretching vibrations of the oxygen-hydrogen (OH) bond, and the carbon-carbon double-bond’s (CC) stretching vibrations, found in the GQD structure [41]. The main peaks found on the FT-IR spectrum of PPy-GQD appeared at 3350, 2941.5, 1652.3, 1512.7, 1251.2, 1398.6, 1110, and 661 cm−1. The peaks at 3350, 1512, and 1398.6 were related to the stretching vibrations of the nitrogen-hydrogen (NH) carbon-nitrogen (CN) and carbon-carbon single-bonds (CC) found in the pyrrole ring [42]. The peak at 2941.5 cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric vibrations of CH2, and the peaks at 1251.2 and 1110 cm−1 were attributed to the deformation of the CH bond and stretching vibrations of the CN in the PPy structure, respectively.

Fig. 2(d) illustrates the formation of polypyrrole in the presence of GQD. Fig. 2(d) is a highly magnified image of the part of the polymer. The GQD was scattered on the surface of polymer impressed the morphology of nanocomposite [43]. Fig. 2(f) and 2(g) show the morphology of GQD which scattered on the surface of polymer and the analysis of the image using Image J software. As a result, the particle size which formed in the spherical shape is about 9.50 nm. The polypyrrole layer was synthesized using electro-polymerization of pyrrole in the presence of SDBS and GQD. The GQD was mixed in the SDBS solution to prevent the aggregation of GQD. SDBS predominantly influences the formation of the polymer in the presence of GQD. SDBS was used to counterbalance the charge density of the polypyrrole radical cations. In order to provide this counterbalance in charge, the SDBS, and GQD provided the anionic charges before oxidation. The anionic charges allowed the electron-movement in polypyrrole and permitted the formation of polypyrrole in its oxidized form. Therefore, the polypyrrole was electropolymerized at the surface of the electrode and the length of PPy increased. During the formation of PPy, the GQD aggregated and scattered in the PPy [44].

Consequently, the PPy-GQD layer was formed on the surface of the gold layer. Various PPy-GQD nanocomposite layers were prepared at different deposition times. The thickness of the layers was measured using a profilometer (AMBIOS, XP-200), and the values ranged between 3.2 nm and 65.2 nm (Table 1 ). When the deposition time increased and the PPy-GQD formed on the surface of gold layer, the thickness of PPy-GQD layer and the length of PPy increased.

The PPy-GQD was deposited on the surface of the gold layer. The thickness and refractive index of gold layer were 46.6 nm and 0.238+i 3.27, respectively. The glass/gold/PPy-GQD system layers were attached to a high-refractive index prism on the side with uncoated glass using a liquid index gel. The distilled water (n = 1.3317) was connected to the layer and the SPR signals were registered using the SPR homemade setup. Fig. 3 (a) displays the SPR signal associated with different thicknesses of the PPy-GQD nanocomposite layer. The SPR signals were analysed using Fresnel’s theory. The experiment was carried out with the limit of about 0.003°. The resonance angle and the reflectance were obtained by minimizing Eq. (7) using minimum root square methods. The resonance angles and reflectance of the SPR signals ranged between 53.316° ± 0.003° to 66.027° ± 0.003° and 0.0128 to 0.4815, respectively. Furthermore, the peak width was increased as the thickness of the nanocomposite increased. The PPy-GQD layer with the 8.1 nm and 12.3 nm thicknesses have the maximum response for the detection of arsenic (see supplement). Thus, the PPy-GQD sensing layer sensor with the 12.3 nm thickness was used for SPR sensor. The parameter values were listed in Table 1. Fig. 3(b) shows the SPR signal corresponding to the 12.3 nm PPy-GQD layer, while the resonance angle which occurred at 55.580 nm was selected as a baseline signal. Fig. 4 (a), 4(b) and 4(c) display the variations in thickness and the differences between real and imaginary parts of the refractive index. Therefore, the thickness of the layer was increased as the deposition time increased and the refractive index of polymer layer depends on the thickness layer because the concentration of GQD and dopant (SDBS) which diffused in the layer increased with an increasing the polymerization time. Hence, the refractive index of the PPy-GQD nanocomposite layer ranged between 1.6895 + i 0.112 to 1.6076+i 0.163, while its thickness increased from 3.2 nm to 65.2 nm. To test the surface homogeneity of the sensing layer, the SPR signals were registered for 12 points of sensing layer surface. The average thickness and refractive index around the experimental area (middle of sensing layer) were constant and the value was chosen to analyse the signal for the detection of arsenic, mercury, and lead (see supplement). The imaginary part (k) of the refractive index increased when the thickness of the PPy-GQD was increased, and the real part (n) of the refractive index decreased by increasing the thickness of the PPy-GQD layer. The variation of the imaginary and real parts of the refractive index was non-compliant and did not align with each other which can be explained using the Kramers–Kroning formula [45].

The glass/gold/PPy-GQD system layer with the 12.3 nm thickness was attached to the prism. The arsenic, mercury, and lead ions solution were loaded separately to the homemade SPR setup. The prism was rotated to 30° at the step of a 0.003°. The experiment was repeated 11 times for each ion and the SPR signals were registered as the time increased. The variations in resonance angle for each concentration of arsenic, mercury, and lead were calculated with drawing a comparison with the baseline (55.58°) angle in order to obtain their sensograms. Fig. 5 (a), 5(b), and 5(c) display the sensograms for arsenic, mercury, and lead ions. They show the ions attached on the surface of sensing layer (association process) before 380 s, and the dotted points are the resonance angle shift values which fit the Langmuir’s first order of absorption model formula (solid lines) [1,17,20] as follows:
(8)
Δ
θ t
= Δ
θ
max t
( 1 - exp
( -
K a
t )
)
,
where
Δ
θ t
,
Δ
θ
max t
,
K a
and
t
are the resonance angle shift with time, the resonance angle shift at the maximum value, a rate constant at association process and the response time of the PPy-GQD sensing layer. Moreover, Fig. 5(a), 5(b), and 5(c) show the ions relesed form sensing layer after 400 s (disosiation process) and the experimental data fit the Eq. (10) as follows:
(9)
Δ
θ t
= Δ
θ
max t
exp
( -
K b
× t )
Where
K b
is a rate constant at dissociation process. The ratio of rate constant at dissociation process and the rate constant at association process calls equlibrium constant. The association time and dissociation time depend on the sensing layer. When the molecule interacts with the sensing layer, the sensing layer adsorbs it with the functional group or free tails. If the number of interaction centres increased the association time decreased. Thus, the response time of sensing layer depends on absorption centre such as functional group. Fig. 5(a), 5(b), and 5(c) show the association process achieved at 380 s and the dissociation process started about 400 s. Therefore, the response time for absorption of ions is about 380 s.

Fig. 5(d) displays the values for the variations in resonance angle shift for different concentrations of arsenic, mercury, and lead, with arsenic having the maximum value at 380 s. The resonance angle shift value is a function of concentration and it fits Langmuir’s formula [46] as follows:
(10)
Δ
θ c
=
( Δ
θ
max c
× C )
1
K d
+ C
,
where
Δ
θ c
,
Δ
θ
max c
,
K d
and
C
are the resonance angle shift with concentration, the resonance angle shifts at the maximum value, the equilibrium constant, and the concentration of arsenic, mercury, and lead, respectively. The pertinent parameters were sorted and presented in Table 2 . The ratio of
K b
and
K a
have been calculated and they matched with the experimental results that show in the Table 2.

The minimum concentration of arsenic ions that could be detected using PPy-GQD sensing layer was about 0.005 ppm, and the resonance angle shift was about 0.0060° which was derived from sensogram (Fig. 5(a)) and Table 2. The experiment was repeated 10 times to draw the sensogram and the average of resonance angle shift for 0.005 ppm concentration was about 0.0060° and the standard deviation was 0.000114°. Consequently, the sensing layer is stability during the experiment. The limit of detection (LOD) defines as a minimum sensitivity of resonance angle shift. In accordance with the literature[48], LOD (
LOD = 3 ∗ ( σ / S ) )
is 3 times larger than ration of standard deviation (
σ
) and sensitivity (S) and in this experiment the sensitivity was achieved about 0.003°. Hence, the LOD of the sensor for detection of arsenic is about 0.112°. As a sensogram result (Fig. 5) the maximum values were achieved at 380 s.

The calculation was repeated to obtain the standard deviation and LOD for detection of mercury and lead. The lowest concentrations of mercury and lead that were detected was 0.005 ppm. The resonance angle shift for detection of mercury and lead ions were achieved at 0.0058° and 0.0056°, respectively. The experiment was separately repeated 10 times for each ions to draw each sensogram and the average of resonance angle shift for detection of mercury and lead were 0.0058° was 0.0056°, respectively. The standard deviation of resonance angle shift for detection of mercury and lead were achieved about 0.000149° and 0.000284°, respectively; and the LOD for detection of mercury was about 0.149° and LOD for detection of lead was achieved 0.1685° which was larger than limit of detection for arsenic and mercury. Consequently, the standard deviation for resonance angle shift and LOD of sensor for detection of arsenic have smallest value. Moreover, the response time of SPR sensor is about 380 s for detection of arsenic, mercury and lead using PPy-GQD layer sensor that is smaller than response time of sensor in Ref [1].

Fig. 5(a), 5(b), and 5(c) show the response time in the range of 10 - 400 s (association process) and the resonance angle shift is about 3.81° to detect the As+3. The response time and the resonance angle shift were improved as a comparison with the pervious activity in the Ref. [1].

The experiment was carried out to obtain the variation of resonance angle shift with different concentration of As+5. Fig. 6 shows the resonance angle shift with concentration of As+5 that is near to As+3 and the resonance angle shift of As+5 and As+3 overlapped in the concentration of As lower than 1 ppm. Consequently, the PPy-GQD is sensitive to detect the As+5 and As+3 with the same sensitivity.

The sensorgram obtained from the mixture is illustrated in Fig. 7 (a). In order to proceed with the experiment, arsenic, mercury, and lead were mixed together in a concentration of 0.5 ppm. As the sensogram curve for the mixture of mercury and lead was lower than that of the mixture of mercury, lead, and arsenic. The sensogram shows that the mixture of As+3 and As+5 is higher than the mixture of mercury, lead, and arsenic while the sensogram related to the mixture of As+3, As+5, mercury and lead is higher than the sensogram of As+3 and As+5. It can be deduced that the affinity of the PPy-GQD sensor layer in detecting arsenic was higher than that of mercury, and lead. Fig. 7(b) shows the variation of resonance angle shift with different percentage of As+3 and As+5, when the percentage of As+3 increased the resonance angle shift also increased and the maximum value of resonance angle shift was achieved when the percentage of As+3 and As+5 were equal (50% As+3 and 50% As+5). The resonance angle shift decreased when the percentage of As+5 also decreased and it is match with Fig. 6.

The experiment was continued to test the polypyrrole sensing layer with the 14 nm thickness. Fig. 8 (a) shows the sensogram for detection of the arsenic ion with the PPy sensing layer. In the previous study, the response of PPy was reported for the detection of lead and mercury [17] and it used to draw the variation of resonance angle shift with the concentration of mercury and lead ions. Fig. 8(b) shows the variation of resonance angle shift with the concentration of arsenic, mercury, and lead. As a result, the response time, sensor’s limit, and maximum resonance angle shift are 783 s, 0.1 ppm, and 0.34° (As), respectively. Consequently, the PPy-GQD can detect arsenic, mercury, and lead during 380 s with sensor’s limit about 0.005 ppm. It means the response time and sensor’s limit of PPy-GQD layer sensor improved 1.95(≈2) time and 20 times than the response time and sensor’s limit of PPy sensing layer. The role of GQD has improved the interaction centres for capping the metal ions and GQD improved the limitation and response time of the sensor.

Following the end of the experiment, the concentration of the ions bound to the sensing layer of the PPy-GQD was analysed. The sensing layers, which had been exposed to 0.5 ppm of each ion, were then separately immersed in distilled water. Subsequently, the original ion-solution samples were also tested in order to measure the remaining concentration of ions after every experiment using ICP-MS. The concentration limit of ICP-MS was 0.001 ppm, and the results of the concentrations are presented in Table 3 .

The degree of adsorption of arsenic, mercury, and lead ions on the surface of PPy-GQD could be estimated from the degree of adsorption as follows [49]:
(11)
E = 100 ×
C i
-
C f
C i
Where the
C i
and
C f
are the concentrations of the ions in the initial and final ion solutions, respectively. The maximum and minimum degrees of adsorption belong to arsenic and lead, respectively. Conclusively, the results verified that the sensing layer had a higher affinity in adsorbing arsenic in comparison to other ions.

Electrodeposition was utilized in order to prepare the PPy-GQD nanocomposite layer. The GQD have hydroxyl and carboxyl groups at the edge of their basal planes, which can be illustrated through the molecular structure of the PPy-GQD, as displayed by Fig. 9 . The GQD and PPy were attached to each other through Van der Waals interactions at the edge of the molecules, as that is where electrons are made available to the polymer. Furthermore, the heavy metal ions interacted with the carboxylic group of GQD, which was an agent between ions and polypyrrole. Hence, it can be concluded that the PPy-GQD layer is capable of adsorbing heavy metals such as arsenic [49]. Because, the particle size of GQD is less than 9.50 nm and the probability of interaction between heavy metals and sensing layer increased and it is advantage of GQD as comparison with other nanoparticles such as gold, silver and magnetic [1] nanoparticles. Thus, the number of interaction centres increased when the GQD was used and the association time decreased, and the sensitivity increased. The response time and the sensitivity of the sensor depends on interaction between the prob medium (such as ions) with the functional group or free tails in sensing layer. Thus, the GQD increased cross-section of interaction between ions and the sensing layer. Therefore, during 380 s the PPy-GQD adsorbed the ions and the association process occurred. The mechanism of sensor is based on variation of optical parameters due to binding the ions with sensing layer. Fig. 9 shows the interaction of arsenic ion with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. The tendency of electron transfer between ions and GQD is the main reason for this interaction. Arsenic is a metalloid and it tends to share the electron with GQD. Therefore, the interaction between GQD and arsenic is more than mercury and lead ions. In accordance with Fresnel’s theory, when the optical parameter change, the resonance angle shift occurs and based on Langmuir’s formula the resonance angle shift is a function of ions concentration. In this study, the binding of ions and GQD was occurred and the Fig. 5(a), 5(b), 5(c) and 5(d) and Fig. 6 authenticated the response of sensor. Therefore, the binding of As to the PPy-GQD has caused that the optical parameters of sensing layer such as refractive index changed, and the resonance angle shift was appeared.

As a comparison the PPy-GQD and polypyrrole-chitosan/CoFe2O4 which was used in previous work [1], it was found that in this study, the sensor layer contains the GQD and it has the main role to detect the arsenic but in polypyrrole-chitosan-CoFe2O4, the chitosan and CoFe2O4 had the main role to detect the heavy metal. Moreover, the preparation of PPy-GQD was done in the one-step while the polypyrrole-chitosan/CoFe2O4 should be prepared in the two steps (The first step is the preparation of CoFe2O4 and after that, the product should be used to prepare the polypyrrole-chitosan-CoFe2O4). Consequently, the PPy-GQD is more suitable for the detection of arsenic ions.

The PPy-GQD sensing layer was formed on the surface of gold layer with the thickness in the range of 3.2 nm to 65.2 nm. The XRD, FT-IR spectra and FE-SEM image verified that GQD were formed and scattered in the PPy-GQD composite layer. The real part of the refractive index for PPy-GQD was decreased from 1.6895 to 1.6076 while the imaginary part of the refractive index for PPy-GQD was increased from 0.112 to 0.163. The prepared layer was utilized to measure low concentrations of As+3, Hg+2, and Pb+2 using SPR technique. The LOD of sensor for detection of arsenic ions was smaller than LOD for detection of mercury and lead and it means the sensitivity of sensor for detection of arsenic is larger than other ions. The degree of adsorption for binding the arsenic to PPy-GQD is about 94.3% and it is larger than degree of adsorption for other ions. In conclusion, it was revealed that the affinity of As+3 in bonding with the PPy-GQD layer was higher than that of the Hg+2 and Pb+2 ions. As a final point, it was observed that the limit sensor and response time were restricted to a concentration of 0.005 ppm and 380 s, respectively.

Amir Reza Sadrolhosseini: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration. Suhaidi Shafie: Validation, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Supervision. Suraya Abdul Rashid: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Visualization. Mohd Adzir Mahdi: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge funding from the Universiti Putra Malaysia, under Geran Putra Berimpak (UPM/800-3/3/1/GPB/2019/9674700) and Institute of Advanced Technology (ITMA) UPM to provide the analytical facilities.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots functionalized gold nanoparticles for sensitive electrochemical detection of heavy metal ions
Electrochim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. L. Ting, S. J. Ee, Arundithi Ananthanarayanan, Kam Chew Leong, Peng Chen Graphene quantum dots functionalized gold nanoparticles for sensitive electrochemical detection of heavy metal ions, Electrochimica Acta , 172(2015) 7-11.
